---
title: "Research"
---

::: {.panel .panel-light}
## Research Agenda

My research asks how scientific mobility, knowledge production, and regional innovation are organized across space. The empirical focus is elite biomedical scientists, but the broader agenda is economic geography: how people, institutions, publications, and cities form spatial systems of scientific work.
:::

::: {.panel .panel-muted}
## Scientific Mobility and Destination Choice

How do particular cities become plausible destinations before an observed move?

I study mobility as a choice among candidate cities, not only as an aggregate flow between places. The destination-choice working paper constructs event-specific candidate sets and measures origin-anchored pre-move publication-location exposure. The interpretation is deliberately limited: among observed mobility events, scientists are more likely to select cities that were already visible in their pre-move publication-location environments, conditional on destination opportunity measures and geographic distance.
:::

::: {.panel .panel-light}
## Knowledge Networks and Regional Innovation

How do scientists' career trajectories connect individual knowledge production with city and regional innovation systems?

This line of work builds on the master's thesis, the accepted article, and ongoing research on elite biomedical scientists. It links career histories, institutional appointments, publication locations, collaboration records, mobility events, and city attributes. The goal is to describe how intercity mobility networks, research platforms, and regional opportunity structures are related, while keeping the interpretation within the limits of the research design.
:::

::: {.panel .panel-muted}
## Longitudinal Relational Data and Spatial Measurement

What kinds of data infrastructure are needed to study scientific careers across people, institutions, publications, and cities?

A large part of my research contribution is measurement work: entity resolution, author-institution-city linkage, candidate-destination construction, city identity checks, and reproducible data-quality audits. The public Scopus Research Data Toolkit reflects this part of the agenda in a safe form, using synthetic fixtures and offline mock transport rather than nonpublic research records.
:::

::: {.panel .panel-dark}
## Methods in Use

The main methods include longitudinal multi-source data construction, social and spatial network analysis, ego-network analysis, conditional logit models for event-level destination choice, WERGM/ERGM specifications for network projects, GIS-based spatial measurement, and reproducible Python/R workflows.
:::
